 {"id":"franken_networkx-clre","title":"[MEDIUM] review: random_kernel_graph rejects fnx create_using constructors with descriptor errors","description":"Review finding from review-only cod pass on 2026-04-22.

Evidence:
- The new `random_kernel_graph` wrapper at `python/franken_networkx/__init__.py:21100` delegates `create_using` directly into `networkx.random_kernel_graph(...)` and then converts the result back.
- Passing FrankenNetworkX graph constructors or instances into `create_using` does not fail closed with a clean NetworkX-style validation path. Repro:
  - `fnx.random_kernel_graph(..., create_using=fnx.Graph)` -> `TypeError: descriptor 'is_directed' for 'franken_networkx.Graph' objects doesn't apply to a 'NoneType' object`
  - `fnx.random_kernel_graph(..., create_using=fnx.Graph())` routes into callback execution and surfaces an unrelated callback-arity error before graph-type validation.

Impact:
- The new parity wrapper still has a broken FNX-native `create_using` path and leaks low-level descriptor errors instead of a stable public contract.

Suggested fix:
- Normalize FNX `create_using` inputs before delegating to NetworkX, or validate/reject them explicitly with a deterministic public error.
- Add differential conformance coverage for `create_using` using both NetworkX graph constructors and FrankenNetworkX graph constructors/instances.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T08:08:53.983661163Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-22T13:14:03.358297536Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T13:13:46.675200709Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["api-parity","generators","review"],"comments":[{"id":58,"issue_id":"franken_networkx-clre","author":"ubuntu","text":"Fixed in latest commit. random_kernel_graph now normalises fnx.{Graph,DiGraph,MultiGraph,MultiDiGraph} class/instance inputs to their nx equivalents before delegating, avoiding the descriptor TypeError from nx's G.is_directed(None) path on Rust-bound classes. nx validation still fires (DiGraph still rejected), error parity maintained.","created_at":"2026-04-22T13:14:03Z"}]}
 {"id":"franken_networkx-cm5","title":"Generators: Scale-free variants (dual BA, extended BA, scale_free_graph)","description":"Generators: Scale-Free Graph Variants

Extended preferential attachment and power-law graph generators beyond basic Barabási-Albert.

## Functions to Implement

- `dual_barabasi_albert_graph(n, m1, m2, p, initial_graph=None, seed=None) -> Graph`: BA variant where each new node attaches m1 edges with probability p, or m2 edges with probability (1-p). Models heterogeneous attachment rates.
- `extended_barabasi_albert_graph(n, m, p, q, initial_graph=None, seed=None) -> Graph`: BA variant with three operations per step: add node+edges (prob p), add edges between existing (prob q), rewire existing edge (prob 1-p-q).
- `scale_free_graph(n, alpha=0.41, beta=0.54, gamma=0.05, delta_in=0.2, delta_out=0, initial_graph=None, seed=None) -> MultiDiGraph`: Directed scale-free graph using Bollobás et al. model with in/out degree preferential attachment.
- `random_powerlaw_tree(n, gamma=3, seed=None, tries=100) -> Graph`: Generate a random tree with power-law degree distribution using a pruned configuration model.
- `random_powerlaw_tree_sequence(n, gamma=3, seed=None, tries=100) -> list`: Generate a degree sequence for a power-law tree (sum=2(n-1), valid tree sequence).
- `gn_graph(n, kernel=None, create_using=None, seed=None) -> DiGraph`: Growing network (GN) graph where new nodes attach to one existing node chosen proportional to kernel(degree). Default kernel is identity (linear preferential attachment).

## Algorithmic Approach

- Dual BA: extend existing Rust BA engine with dual-mode attachment selection per step.
- Extended BA: three-phase step logic (add-node, add-edge, rewire) with preferential attachment for target selection.
- scale_free_graph: Bollobás directed model - maintain in-degree and out-degree lists, select source/target with probability proportional to degree + delta offset.
- random_powerlaw_tree: generate power-law degree sequence via `powerlaw_sequence`, check validity (sum=2(n-1), all ≥1), build tree via Prüfer sequence or configuration model. Retry up to `tries` times.
- gn_graph: sequential node addition, each new node connects to one existing node with probability proportional to kernel(in_degree).

## Performance Targets

- Extend existing Rust BA infrastructure where possible for O(n·m) construction.
- scale_free_graph: O(n) expected time using degree-list sampling.
- random_powerlaw_tree: may need multiple tries; each try is O(n).
- Target: 100K node dual/extended BA graphs in < 2 seconds.

## Edge Cases

- `p + q > 1` in extended BA → raise ValueError.
- `m1 == m2` in dual BA → degenerates to standard BA.
- `alpha + beta + gamma != 1` in scale_free_graph → raise ValueError.
- `initial_graph` with fewer nodes than required (need at least m+1 for BA variants).
- `random_powerlaw_tree` failing to find valid sequence after `tries` → raise NetworkXError.
- `n = 0` or `n = 1` edge cases.
- `kernel=None` defaults to identity function in gn_graph.

## Test Requirements

- Unit tests: correct node/edge counts, graph type (directed vs undirected).
- Degree distribution tests: fit power-law exponent on large graphs (n≥10000), verify γ is within expected range.
- Reproducibility tests: same seed → identical graph (node-for-node, edge-for-edge).
- NX cross-validation: compare with NetworkX output for identical seeds and parameters.
- Property tests: dual BA with p=1 equivalent to BA(m1), p=0 equivalent to BA(m2).
- Extended BA: verify all three operations occur with approximately correct frequencies over many steps.
- scale_free_graph: verify in-degree and out-degree distributions are both power-law.
- random_powerlaw_tree: output is a tree (connected, n-1 edges).

## Implementation Notes

- Extend existing Rust BA implementation in the crate (reuse preferential attachment sampling).
- Python wrapper layer for API compatibility.
- scale_free_graph returns MultiDiGraph (parallel edges possible).
- gn_graph is a directed graph model.
- Depends on: core graph types, existing BA Rust infrastructure, numpy for random sampling.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-21T19:20:21.711622106Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-23T06:11:17.367892098Z","closed_at":"2026-03-23T06:11:17.367368888Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["generators","tier-2"]}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-deh","title":"Drawing & Visualization: draw_networkx variants, LaTeX export, network_text","description":"## Scope
Implement ~15 drawing/visualization functions:
- `draw_networkx` — main drawing function (matplotlib)
- `draw_networkx_nodes` — draw nodes only
- `draw_networkx_edges` — draw edges only
- `draw_networkx_labels` — draw node labels
- `draw_networkx_edge_labels` — draw edge labels
- `draw_circular` — draw with circular layout
- `draw_kamada_kawai` — draw with Kamada-Kawai layout
- `draw_planar` — draw with planar layout
- `draw_random` — draw with random positions
- `draw_shell` — draw with shell layout
- `draw_spectral` — draw with spectral layout
- `draw_spring` — draw with spring layout
- `draw_forceatlas2` — draw with ForceAtlas2 layout
- `write_latex` / `to_latex` / `to_latex_raw` — export graph to LaTeX/TikZ
- `network_text` — ASCII art representation of graph

## Implementation Approach
- Most draw_* functions delegate to matplotlib with layout computation
- Can wrap NetworkX drawing directly for many of these
- network_text: ASCII tree/graph rendering, pure Python
- LaTeX export: generate TikZ code, pure Python string generation

## Implementation Location
- Python: `python/franken_networkx/drawing/`

## Test Requirements
- Verify draw functions don't error on valid graphs
- Verify LaTeX output is valid TikZ
- Verify network_text produces readable output
- Visual regression tests (optional)","status":"closed","priority":4,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-21T19:14:33.237335091Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-22T03:36:38.812894650Z","closed_at":"2026-03-22T03:36:38.812214806Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["drawing","tier-4","visualization"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"franken_networkx-deh","depends_on_id":"franken_networkx-l85","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T19:16:02.736514279Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-eup","title":"Centrality Extras: numpy variants, incremental closeness, current-flow subsets","description":"## Scope
Implement 5 advanced centrality functions:
- eigenvector_centrality_numpy(G, weight='weight', max_iter=50, tol=0) — eigenvector centrality using numpy.linalg.eig instead of power iteration. Faster for dense graphs, O(n^3) for eigendecomposition vs O(n*m*k) for power iteration.
- katz_centrality_numpy(G, alpha=0.1, beta=1.0, weight='weight') — Katz centrality via matrix inversion: x = (I - alpha*A)^{-1} * beta. Requires alpha < 1/spectral_radius(A). O(n^3) for matrix inversion.
- incremental_closeness_centrality(G, u, prev_cc=None, insertion=True, wt_attr=None) — efficiently update closeness centrality after a single edge insertion/deletion. Instead of O(n*(n+m)) full recompute, uses BFS delta updates. O(n+m) per update.
- current_flow_betweenness_centrality_subset(G, sources, targets, normalized=True, weight=None, dtype=float, solver='full') — current-flow betweenness restricted to (source,target) subsets. Requires solving Laplacian system for each source-target pair.
- edge_current_flow_betweenness_centrality_subset(G, sources, targets) — edge variant of above.

## Implementation Strategy
- numpy variants: pure Python with numpy.linalg, return same dict format as iterative versions
- incremental: BFS from both endpoints of changed edge, compute distance deltas, update closeness scores
- current-flow subset: build Laplacian, solve L*x = b for each source-target pair, accumulate flow through each node/edge

## Dependencies
- Depends on existing Laplacian matrix functions (already implemented)
- current-flow variants need scipy.sparse.linalg.spsolve

## Test Requirements
- numpy variants: match iterative versions to within tolerance on same graph
- incremental: result matches full recompute after same edge operation
- current-flow subset: total flow through intermediate nodes is conserved
- NX cross-validation on path, cycle, star, Petersen graphs","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-22T17:34:38.051618164Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-23T07:29:22.704252563Z","closed_at":"2026-03-23T07:29:22.703698784Z","close_reason":"Implemented 5 centrality extras: eigenvector_centrality_numpy, katz_centrality_numpy, incremental_closeness_centrality, current_flow_betweenness_centrality_subset, edge_current_flow_betweenness_centrality_subset.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["algorithms","centrality"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"franken_networkx-eup","depends_on_id":"franken_networkx-v3y","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-22T17:42:04.259127891Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-ndm3","title":"NX-REPLACE: Graph products — cartesian, tensor, strong, lexicographic, corona, modular, rooted","description":"## Functions (7) — graph product implementations
1. cartesian_product — G□H: (g1,h1)~(g2,h2) iff g1=g2,h1~h2 OR g1~g2,h1=h2
2. tensor_product — G×H: (g1,h1)~(g2,h2) iff g1~g2 AND h1~h2
3. strong_product — G⊠H: union of cartesian and tensor products
4. lexicographic_product — G∘H: (g1,h1)~(g2,h2) iff g1~g2 OR (g1=g2 AND h1~h2)
5. corona_product — each node in G gets a complete copy of H
6. modular_product — (g1,h1)~(g2,h2) iff (g1~g2 AND h1~h2) OR (g1≁g2 AND h1≁h2)
7. rooted_product — each node in G gets a copy of H rooted at specified root

## Implementation plan
All products follow a common pattern:
1. Create nodes as (g, h) tuples for all g ∈ V(G), h ∈ V(H)
2. Add edges based on product-specific adjacency rules
3. Preserve node/edge attributes from source graphs

Rust implementation: create a generic product framework that takes an edge predicate, then instantiate for each product type. Nodes stored as 'g|h' strings in Rust, converted to Python tuples in the binding.

## Testing requirements
Create tests/python/test_graph_products_parity.py:
- test_cartesian_product_paths: P2□P3 is a 2x3 grid
- test_cartesian_product_matches_nx: compare nodes/edges on 3+ graph pairs
- test_tensor_product_matches_nx: compare on K3×K3, P3×C4
- test_strong_product_is_union: verify G⊠H = G□H ∪ G×H
- test_strong_product_matches_nx: compare on small graphs
- test_lexicographic_product_matches_nx: compare on P3∘K2
- test_corona_product_matches_nx: compare node/edge counts
- test_modular_product_matches_nx: compare on small graphs
- test_rooted_product_matches_nx: compare on P3 rooted at 0
- test_node_keys_are_tuples: verify product nodes are Python tuples
- test_edge_attributes_preserved: verify source attrs carry through

Run: pytest tests/python/test_graph_products_parity.py -v --tb=long

## Files to modify
- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs: add 7 product functions
- crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs: add 7 bindings with tuple node keys
- python/franken_networkx/__init__.py: rewire 7 functions
- tests/python/test_graph_products_parity.py: new test file (11+ tests)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-03T00:18:40.268274007Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-03T14:01:43.595286955Z","closed_at":"2026-04-03T14:01:43.595105031Z","close_reason":"Replaced graph product NetworkX round-trips with direct FNX implementations and added parity coverage","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"franken_networkx-ndm3","depends_on_id":"franken_networkx-okpa","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-03T00:21:20.308135797Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-poy","title":"Similarity: SimRank, Panther, edit distance optimization","description":"## Scope
Implement 5 graph similarity and edit distance functions:
- simrank_similarity(G, source=None, target=None, importance_factor=0.9, max_iterations=100, tolerance=1e-4) — iterative SimRank computation. Two nodes are similar if their neighbors are similar. sim(a,b) = C * sum(sim(n_a, n_b)) / (|N(a)| * |N(b)|). O(n^2 * k) per iteration, k iterations. Returns dict of dicts or single float.
- panther_similarity(G, source, k=5, path_length=5, c=0.5, seed=None) — random-walk based similarity. Sample random walks of given length from source, count co-occurrence with each target. O(k * path_length) per source.
- panther_vector_similarity(G, source, k=5, path_length=5, c=0.5, seed=None) — vector variant returning similarity scores as dict.
- optimal_edit_paths(G1, G2, node_match=None, edge_match=None) — find all edit paths achieving the minimum graph edit distance. Returns (node_edit_path, edge_edit_path, cost). Uses A* search on edit operation space. Exponential worst case.
- optimize_edit_paths(G1, G2, node_match=None, edge_match=None) — iterator yielding progressively better edit paths. Same algorithm as optimal but yields intermediate results.

## Implementation Strategy
- SimRank: iterative matrix update, pure Python with numpy for matrix operations
- Panther: random walk sampling with collections.Counter, pure Python
- Edit distance: extend existing graph_edit_distance infrastructure (if available) or implement Hungarian algorithm for node mapping + edge matching

## Test Requirements
- SimRank: self-similarity = 1.0, symmetric (sim(a,b) = sim(b,a)), convergence within tolerance
- Panther: source has highest self-similarity, scores are non-negative
- Edit paths: cost matches graph_edit_distance, edit operations are valid
- NX cross-validation on small graphs (n <= 8)","status":"closed","priority":4,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-22T17:34:38.337874607Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-23T07:35:01.243959829Z","closed_at":"2026-03-23T07:35:01.243200421Z","close_reason":"Implemented 4 similarity: simrank_similarity, panther_similarity, optimal_edit_paths, optimize_edit_paths.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["algorithms"]}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-pqcf","title":"BACKEND: Make installed NetworkX backend dispatch actually route to FrankenNetworkX","description":"## Gap
Validation for `franken_networkx-6z8n` showed that the package installs the `networkx.backends` entry point, but direct NetworkX dispatch still fails:

```python
import networkx as nx
import franken_networkx
nx.shortest_path(nx.path_graph(8), 0, 7, backend="franken_networkx")
```

Current runtime error:
`"shortest_path" is not implemented by "franken_networkx" backend.`

## What works today
- `franken_networkx.backend.BackendInterface.convert_from_nx / convert_to_nx`
- `BackendInterface.can_run / should_run`
- Calling backend methods directly on FrankenNetworkX graph objects

## What is broken
- Installed-package automatic NetworkX dispatch path for supported algorithms
- `nx.shortest_path(..., backend="franken_networkx")` on a clean installed environment
- `nx.shortest_path.backends` does not advertise `franken_networkx` after install

## Likely surfaces
- `python/franken_networkx/backend.py`
- backend registration / entry-point compatibility with NetworkX 3.6 dispatch expectations
- packaging metadata if entry-point load shape is wrong

## Acceptance
- Supported algorithms dispatch successfully from NetworkX with `backend="franken_networkx"`
- At minimum verify `shortest_path`, `pagerank`, and `connected_components`
- Add an installed-environment regression test so this does not silently break again","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-06T18:59:55.061732371Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-06T21:29:44.031105212Z","closed_at":"2026-04-06T21:29:44.030837064Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-w7ge","title":"NX-REPLACE: Misc algorithms — euler, cycles, hashing, coloring, similarity, etc.","description":"## Functions to replace (22)

Graph hashing:

Similarity/distance:

10. panther_vector_similarity (__init__.py:7170) — vector variant
11. cd_index (__init__.py:7229) — consolidation-disruption index

Misc:
12. local_bridges (__init__.py:1345) — edges whose removal increases distance
13. complete_to_chordal_graph (__init__.py:6938) — chordal completion
14. effective_graph_resistance (__init__.py:7201) — Kirchhoff index
15. goldberg_radzik (__init__.py:7237) — shortest paths with negative weights
16. communicability_exp (__init__.py:7162) — matrix exponential
17. local_constraint (__init__.py:7123) — Burt's constraint measure
18. is_perfect_graph (__init__.py:7486) — perfect graph recognition
19. k_factor (__init__.py:7541) — k-regular spanning subgraph
20. spectral_graph_forge (__init__.py:7550) — spectral graph generation
21. mycielskian (__init__.py:7293) — Mycielski graph construction
22. random_powerlaw_tree_sequence (__init__.py:8299) — power-law degree sequence
